title: Markdown Frontmatter Knowledge Graphs
subtitle: Markdown can become a lightweight knowledge graph when every note carries typed metadata and explicit links.
summary: KRAIL builds markdown-frontmatter entities, edges, topic documents, search, think envelopes, and integrity artifacts.

artifact.title: Files become typed nodes and edges
artifact.description: The graph demo maps nicely to markdown entities connected by source, task, topic, and evidence relationships.

contentBlocks.content:
A markdown note can carry structured frontmatter:

```yaml
type: source-note
topic: transit-analysis
project: rutgers-bus-analysis
status: reviewed
links:
  - route-loop-time-estimation
  - campus-bus-load-curves
```

The prose remains readable, but the metadata lets software query and connect it.

sections.heading: Why Frontmatter Works
body: Frontmatter gives plain files just enough structure. You can keep the benefits of Git, grep, and human-readable notes while adding machine-readable type, status, source, and relationship fields.
This is useful for agent work because the agent can search, cite, update, and validate context without needing a heavyweight database for every task.

sections.heading: Graph Edges
body: Edges can be explicit links, shared tags, source references, task dependencies, or generated backlinks. The important part is that relationships should have meaning, not just visual beauty.
A graph is useful when it answers questions: which claims depend on this source, which tasks are blocked, which topics have stale evidence, and which outputs were verified.

rows: Report, chart, code output, wiki page

pitfalls: Using tags as decoration instead of queryable structure.
Letting metadata drift from the prose.
Depending on generated backlinks without validating them.

quiz.question: Why use frontmatter in markdown notes?
options: To make notes queryable while preserving readable files
To prevent Git from tracking files
quiz.explanation: Frontmatter adds structure without giving up plain-text workflows.
